edges.explanation: Taxes at record highs yet services still failing proves that even current revenue is insufficient, let alone after tax locks prevent further rises
Record taxes and failing services is the direct evidence of the gap between what's pledged and what's needed
£50bn/yr in unexpected debt interest further constrains the fiscal space available for public services
Debt servicing costs absorbing £50bn/yr means manifestos that don't account for this are even more dishonest about available spending room
Two-thirds of revenue locked off by both major parties proves services cannot be sustainably funded without raising the big three
Locking two-thirds of revenue is the mechanism that forces the dishonesty the claim identifies
The scale of revenue locked — two-thirds — is what makes tax locks the most damaging constraint on governance
£41bn/yr from fiscal drag being 4x the NI cut is the direct evidence that both parties are raising taxes while claiming not to
The £41bn fiscal drag figure makes the case for ending the threshold freeze and indexing to inflation
£36bn total gap but mostly from small businesses proves that tax gap claims are overstated and not from the targets politicians imply
The gap existing at £36bn but being underexploited justifies the HMRC investment — the revenue is there but requires capacity to collect
The 10-20% vs 20-45% disparity creating reclassification incentives is the economic case for CGT equalisation
The rate disparity and £10-15bn revenue estimate directly size and justify the CGT equalisation policy
A 4:1 tax ratio on a 100:1 value difference using 1991 values is the most vivid evidence that council tax is indefensible
The absurdity of 1991 bands on 2024 values directly justifies proportional property tax reform
£5-18 return per £1 proves tax gap revenue requires investment in HMRC not just political will
The £5-18 return ratio makes HMRC investment the highest-ROI policy in the entire fiscal domain
75% public support suggests political feasibility of wealth taxation may be higher than parties assume
56% support for council tax reform suggests the political toxicity may be overstated
The Truss mini-budget market crash is the definitive UK evidence that unfunded tax cuts do not generate growth
Markets punishing unfunded promises demonstrates that fiscal dishonesty has real economic consequences
Even the £17-40bn reform range leaves a gap against £20-25bn NHS + £5bn care + £5-10bn housing needs, proving big three taxes may be needed
The revenue range from reforms maps directly to the cross-domain spending needs, making the dependency chain visible

The evidence rejecting the assumption that tax cuts pay for themselves proves the fiscal gap is real — if growth cannot close the gap, then the manifesto spending promises require either tax rises or cuts that no party will name
The failure of the Laffer argument is evidence that tax lock pledges based on growth assumptions are dishonest
Tax gap being real but requiring HMRC investment to collect supports the compliance funding policy
CGT inequality being unjustified means potential revenue is being left on the table while parties claim there's no money
Strong economic case for equalisation directly justifies the CGT reform policy
Council tax being indefensible on every metric justifies proportional property tax reform
Services not fundable without big three taxes is the core evidence that manifestos are dishonest
The impossibility of funding services without the big three is what makes tax locks so damaging
The fiscal impossibility is what creates the dependency chain from failing services to fiscal dishonesty
Scrapping tax locks is the direct response to the manifesto dishonesty that C01 identifies
Tax locks being the most damaging innovation is the justification for the policy to abolish them
Ending fiscal drag depends on the claim that stealth taxation through frozen thresholds is dishonest
CGT equalisation is the largest single revenue measure to break the dependency chain from failing services to fiscal dishonesty
CGT policy depends on the strong economic case for equalisation being correct
Council tax reform depends on the assumption that the current system is indefensible and reform is justified
Land value tax pilot depends partly on the finding that annual wealth taxation faces challenges, making LVT a better alternative
HMRC investment depends on the finding that the tax gap requires capacity investment to close, not just political will
Further NI cuts and pledging no rises contradicts the evidence that CGT equalisation and other revenue measures are needed
Locking all major taxes is the exact behaviour the scrap-tax-locks policy targets
Labour's no-rise pledge on the big three is itself a tax lock that the evidence says should be abolished
Labour raised CGT but not to income tax parity, falling short of the evidence-based full equalisation
Lib Dem targeting £7.2bn from tax gap aligns with HMRC investment approach though overstates recoverable amount
Green CGT equalisation matches the evidence-based policy exactly
Green carbon tax aligns with the carbon-tax-with-dividend policy
Cutting corporation tax and abolishing IHT reduces revenue in the opposite direction to the evidence-based reform
£50bn spending cuts as an alternative to honest taxation contradicts the evidence that services are already underfunded
Record tax burden yet failing services means further tax cuts will worsen service delivery, contradicting the Conservative fiscal programme
The Truss mini-budget demonstrated exactly what happens when unfunded tax cuts are attempted
Locking two-thirds of revenue while claiming to fund services through growth is the dishonesty the IFS identifies
CGT equalisation creates the political and legal framework that makes IHT loophole closure more coherent — both address wealth-related tax avoidance
CGT reform alongside council tax reform creates a coherent wealth-related tax reform package
Scrapping tax locks is a prerequisite for honest fiscal reform — without it, CGT equalisation and other measures face political constraints
IHT loophole closure depends on the finding that reforming existing wealth taxes is more effective than new annual wealth taxes
Carbon tax with dividend depends on the cross-domain finding that environmental taxation is one of the few remaining progressive revenue sources outside the big three

cross_domain_edges.explanation: The NHS £20-25bn funding gap is the largest single demand on the fiscal space that tax reform aims to create
Fiscal dishonesty directly supports the NHS finding that every manifesto falls short on funding
Council housebuilding at £5-10bn/yr depends on the fiscal space that tax reform creates
Carbon tax with dividend is the fiscal instrument that supports reforming wholesale electricity pricing to decouple from gas
Council tax reform addresses the regressive property taxation that compounds housing affordability failures
£41bn fiscal drag squeezing real incomes directly contributes to welfare pressure on lower-income households
